originalTitle: Shortsighted stock market can no longer brush off war: 'It's too hard to ignore $100 oil'
summary: After staying flat as the U.S.-Iran war heated up again, equities tumbled on Thursday after oil prices crossed $100 per barrel.
content:
Major U.S. stock indexes tumbled on Thursday as investors began to price in the consequences of a renewed and prolonged conflict in the Middle East.

While the U.S. has conducted strikes against Iran 12 nights in a row — sending both oil prices and Treasury yields higher — domestic equities had mostly brushed off the idea of the war between the two countries heating up again, staying flat while oil jumped.

That changed on Thursday, though, when Brent Crude futures jumped above $100 per barrel and the 10-year Treasury yield broke through 4.7%, hitting its highest level since January 2025 , after reports of attacks against tankers off the coast of Saudi Arabia. The S&P 500 headed for its biggest decline in a month.

"These problems became too big to ignore," said Steve Sosnick, chief strategist at Interactive Brokers, about the move in stocks on Thursday. "It's too hard to ignore $100 oil. It's too hard to ignore 10-year rates that are above 4.70%. It's too hard for the stock market to ignore 30-year rates that are solidly above 5%."

Western Texas Intermediate Crude futures jumped 6% to $92 per barrel, up more than 28% from lows below $70 per barrel they hit earlier this month. The S&P 500 is now down about 2% since the consecutive evening strikes by the U.S. began on July 12.

In March, after the U.S-Iran war began, the S&P 500 fell more than 7.5% at its low point as oil surged nearly 70% and investors worried about stagflation, where higher energy prices would reignite inflation while elevated costs at the gas pump would weigh on consumer spending.

A series of de-escalation announcements between the two countries and a reignited faith in the artificial intelligence trade led the S&P to ferociously rebound in April and May, even as hostilities at various times continued .

The big factor, however, was a bet that President Donald Trump would find an off-ramp to end the war rather than face the economic and political consequences of a prolonged conflict.

"We have consistently argued since 2nd half of March to use the equity weakness brought on by the Iran conflict to buy into, as the off-ramp and the eventual deal were likely, in our view," wrote JPMorgan equity strategists in a note earlier this month. "The risks of renewed flareups remain, but we believe one should keep using any dips on the back of adverse geopolitical headlines in order to add."

Sameer Samana, head of global equities and real assets at Wells Fargo Investment Institute, now thinks traders need to reconsider the economic fears they had in March.

Investors should be worried about both higher inflation and the impact higher gas prices may have on consumers, he said. Samana added the reignited conflict is a reason to prepare for a larger drawdown in equities.

Sosnick said that stocks on Thursday were also likely pricing in a tighter borrowing environment for companies. Chances for a rate hike by the Federal Reserve next week according to CME'S FedWatch tool are up to almost 38%, while odds for a hike at the central bank's September meeting are at more than 80%.

A week ago, those odds were priced at about 12% and 53%, respectively

Back in March, many analysts were surprised that the stock market didn't react more to the conflict initially, and concluded that the U.S. economy was in better shape than in the past to handle energy shocks. That's a bet that Michael Tanney, CEO at investment advisory firm Pereon Wealth, is taking again.

"In the short term, the elevated spike is more meaningful to the headlines than client portfolios," Tanney said. "If we have a sustained price above $120, that's the breaking point where you'll see serious trickle down effects."

— CNBC's Deena Zaidi, Tanaya Macheel, Ananya Chetia contributed reporting
chineseTitle: 目光短浅的股市无法再忽视战争：“很难忽视 100 美元的石油”
chineseSummary: 由于美伊战争再次升温，股市保持平稳，周四油价突破每桶 100 美元后暴跌。
chineseContent:
由于投资者开始消化中东新一轮长期冲突的后果，美国主要股指周四暴跌。

尽管美国连续 12 个晚上对伊朗进行打击，导致油价和国债收益率双双走高，但国内股市基本上没有理会两国战争再次升温的想法，保持平稳，而油价则上涨。

不过，这种情况在周四发生了变化，在有报道称沙特阿拉伯沿海油轮遭到袭击后，布伦特原油期货跃升至每桶 100 美元以上，10 年期国债收益率突破 4.7%，触及 2025 年 1 月以来的最高水平。标准普尔 500 指数录得一个月来最大跌幅。

盈透证券（Interactive Brokers）首席策略师史蒂夫·索斯尼克（Steve Sosnick）在谈到周四的股市走势时表示：“这些问题变得太大而不容忽视。” “很难忽视 100 美元的油价。很难忽视高于 4.70% 的 10 年期利率。股市也很难忽视稳定高于 5% 的 30 年期利率。”

西德克萨斯中质原油期货上涨 6%，至每桶 92 美元，较本月初触及的每桶 70 美元以下的低点上涨逾 28%。自 7 月 12 日美国连续晚间罢工以来，标准普尔 500 指数已下跌约 2%。

3月份，美伊战争爆发后，标准普尔500指数在低点下跌超过7.5%，原因是油价飙升近70%，投资者担心滞胀，即能源价格上涨将重新引发通胀，而加油站成本上升将拖累消费者支出。

两国之间的一系列缓和局势公告以及对人工智能贸易的重新燃起的信心导致标准普尔指数在 4 月和 5 月猛烈反弹，尽管不同时期的敌对行动仍在继续。

然而，最重要的因素是，人们押注唐纳德·特朗普总统会找到结束战争的出口，而不是面对长期冲突的经济和政治后果。

摩根大通股票策略师在本月早些时候的一份报告中写道：“自 3 月 2 日起，我们一直主张利用伊朗冲突带来的股市疲软来买入，因为我们认为，退出和最终协议是可能的。” “新一轮爆发的风险依然存在，但我们认为，人们应该继续利用不利的地缘政治头条新闻带来的任何下跌来增加风险。”

富国银行投资研究所全球股票和实物资产主管 Sameer Samana 现在认为交易者需要重新考虑他们 3 月份的经济担忧。

他表示，投资者应该担心通胀上升以及汽油价格上涨可能对消费者产生的影响。萨马纳补充说，重新燃起的冲突是为股市大幅下跌做好准备的一个原因。

索斯尼克表示，周四的股市也可能反映了企业借贷环境收紧的情况。根据芝商所的 FedWatch 工具，美联储下周加息的可能性高达近 38%，而央行 9 月会议上加息的可能性则超过 80%。

一周前，这些赔率分别约为 12% 和 53%

早在三月份，许多分析师就对股市最初没有对冲突做出更多反应感到惊讶，并得出结论认为，美国经济比过去更能应对能源冲击。投资咨询公司 Pereon Wealth 的首席执行官迈克尔·坦尼 (Michael Tanney) 再次下了这个赌注。

坦尼说：“从短期来看，上涨对头条新闻比对客户投资组合更有意义。” “如果我们的价格持续高于 120 美元，那就是临界点，你会看到严重的涓滴效应。”

— CNBC 的 Deena Zaidi、Tanaya Macheel、Ananya Chetia 贡献了报道
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于财经市场信号，适合用于观察利率、汇率、股债商品、金融机构和宏观政策变化。
